speaker: interviewee
text: What else?

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you'll talk about the camps-- Oranienburg and-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this was a-- this were already when--

speaker: interviewer
text: Later, later. I know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Later, maybe.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oranienburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good. OK. I think I'm going to be all right once we start.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have to keep like this?

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh-- oh, until-- only when we're finished. He'll stop, and then we'll start the interview. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: No charge?

speaker: interviewer
text: Is Chaia your Jewish name or your Polish name?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. I don't know if you noticed, but I'm losing my shadows.

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish name is Chaia. And I call-- they call me Chaia, because I was born here. Korets was a very Jewish town.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's what I want you to talk about.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a Hasidic town-- all Hasidism.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's what I want you to talk about. I want you to talk about all that too.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is an interview with Clara Skaletzky, S-K-A-L-E-T-Z-K-Y, who was born as Chaia, C-H-A-I-A, Bren, B-R-E-N, her maiden name. She was born in Korets, Poland on October 15, 1924. I'm Donna Kanter, D-O-N-N-A, K-A-N-T-E-R. And we're in the town of Encino, in the city of Los Angeles, California, in the United States of America. And today's date is January 24, 1995.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it's in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the language of our interview is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Five seconds.

speaker: interviewer
text: Clara--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: --tell me your first name--

speaker: interviewee
text: My--

speaker: interviewer
text: --your full name and your age.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Clara Skaletzky-- born Chaia Bren-- and 19-- October, 1924-- October 15, 1924. I made a mistake-- and-- and in Korets, Rovna, Oblast. It's Ukrainian SSR right now-- yesterday. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: You're 70 years old now?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm 70 years old right now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your childhood in Korets.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born to a very orthodox family. My father was a Hasid. He was a very orthodox Jew. My mother was the same thing. And he was a businessman. He had a grocery market. And usually, also, he sold wholesale and retail. And he was-- I was raised by my parents very nice, in a Jewish way. They liked-- they kept up all the holidays-- Shabbat-- to teach us to-- to-- and all the-- the-- all the Jewish-- the Jewish holidays we kept very, very holy. We didn't do nothing in that days. My father used to go praying to the synagogue. My mother was-- we'd been together. We-- we make the-- my mother used to cook all the meals for the holiday-- special meals, and we eat together. And they raised us in a-- in a very good atmosphere. And I was a very happy child when I was young-- my sister with myself. We had a lot of pictures, but the pictures has gone, because everything is perished. And we had some relatives-- orthodox, too. We used to come and visit them. They used to visit us. They used to-- my father had a sister in a-- in a different town. I used to take-- my father used to take me all the time to my aunt and my cousins. And they used to make-- they used to be very good. They are a-- they had business. They had-- they had pretty good-- well-off. And we used to go there for Shabbos. And we spent times with my parents, with-- with-- mostly with my father. My father used to take us. My mother used to stay home with my sister who was about four years about younger, so she couldn't travel with us. And I spent time with my-- with my father. I loved my father very much. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: That's-- what else? What can I say? That was my childhood-- a very happy childhood.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to Hebrew school. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm emotional.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then-- then, later on, I went to a regular school. They sent me to-- to a public school. And I learned the language-- the Polish language-- a little bit, and-- and Ukrainian language-- in the school, and arithmetic. And-- and I finished public school. Seven years I went to that school. And I had a special-- a teacher in the house to teach me all the religious things-- Jewish. And then, that was my childhood-- a very happy childhood. I had a happy childhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your sister, what her name was.

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister is-- her name is Rachel-- Rachel, in Jewish-- the name-- same last name, Bren. And she was, of course, she was younger-- four years than I. And so I used to be all the time the big shot in the house. And she was a-- a happy a child, too-- all the time. And we went through the same thing-- went to school with-- the same schools, went to Hebrew school, and went to the school-- the public school-- finished public school. And we growed up together. And we loved each other. Our mother was-- my-- our mother was very devoted to us. She teached us all the good things-- how to be a housewife, how to be-- cook-- and to cook, to sew. She's-- my mother used to said-- say to me-- you have-- you have to know everything. You have to know everything, but with-- well, God's help, we wouldn't have to do it. You're not going to do it. But you have to know everything from scratch. You have to know how to sew. You have to know how to cook. You have to know how to clean. And she teach how this way. And she brought us up this-- this way. And this-- this is the way we are. We-- I owe myself. I make myself all the time. In fact, I got married, and I took care-- took care of my-- my house. At least--

speaker: interviewer
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE] Yes. You--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I'm not to--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you--

speaker: interviewee
text: --disconnected.

speaker: interviewer
text: --you continued--

speaker: interviewee
text: I continued later on after-- after-- for Poland. Later on, I went to my-- I was off a couple-- one year I think I was off till the-- the Russian Army came-- the Red Army came into-- they separated Poland in two parts. So they came in. And then, I went again to school. Before the schooling, my mother send me to a special school to-- to learn how to sew-- how to sew, how to knit, everything. She says, you have to know everything. It's very-- very important for a woman to know everything. You don't have to do it everything, but you-- it's important to know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- I did it. I learned everything good. And I-- and I followed my mother-- my mother's advice. Whatever she told me, I did it-- and my father's advice. That's the way I growed up in this kind of environment. The town itself was a very old town-- 600 years old. And it's-- the town had-- had a lot of Jewish things inside. In fact, a rabbi, a big rabbi, a famous rabbi, Pinchas-- Pinchas Shapiro is-- is he-- he is all over on the-- in the libraries. He-- even in the United State, I saw him in the-- wrote his name in the library that he was very famous. We had some-- in the town was a couple of-- can I wipe my own tear?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I was emotional-- I got to wipe. OK. But as long as they wait to stop-- if you don't mind. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Like I said already, I come from a very religious home. My-- my father was-- my grandfather was a teacher. He was teaching people to pray. He was sitting in the-- in the synagogue in-- and studying all the time-- Torah. My own grandmother was the business lady. She was making money. And he was-- and they was 17 children-- she had 17 children, my grandmother. But not all of them survived, because she couldn't nurse them. She had special woman, [NON-ENGLISH] to nurse the children, but they couldn't-- they couldn't survive-- survived only five. And my father was the only son-- the only son survived. And, of course, he had three sisters. And he was raised very-- very carefully raised by his parents. He was very-- the only son, you know, a son-- everybody's pleased for a son-- and went to schools. He was very religious. He knew all the-- he went to a private teacher to teach him all, you know, arithmetic, and our world things. And the other things it was in Hebrew-- everything in Hebrew, not in-- so he-- he was-- he was very educated-- writing and writing, and then-- very educated, my son-- my father. And I loved him very much. [CRYING] My mother was the same way. She comes from a very religious home. My grandfather, had-- he was in business in-- in lumber business-- lumber business. My grandmother had a-- a market in a village. In fact, this village-- I was saved. This village saved my life. They had like a-- they had-- my grandmother had a store, a general merchandise store. And my grandfather was working in the lumber-- in the lumber yard. They had 12 children. Not all of them survived, either. It was in the old days in Russia. It was very hard to-- to raise children. They-- they got sick, you know, the-- the children sickness. And they died. And survived about five or six children-- five children survived, too. So my mother was one of them. And she married my father after the World War I. And then, the-- she left Russia over there-- Russia-- the village. And then other side was Poland, because we was living on the border, two kilometers from-- from the Russian-Polish border.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she never could go back. She left her sisters there, with nieces, with nephews, with cousins in Russia. All of them perished. Nobody is alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she was-- and she married my-- my father. And she lived in Poland up till the World War II. That's-- that's then I want to tell you about my mother's family and my father's family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: We came from very orthodox people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Korets itself was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Korets himself was a town-- a Jewish town, a very-- a very nice town-- a Jewish town. And the people was working people, a few business people, and a few-- and working people-- hard-working people. They wasn't afraid for work. But the problem is, they didn't have where work-- a lot of unemployment there. People would go around doing things what they didn't supposed to do-- as long as can-- you can make a dollar, you can make a-- to-- to make a living. And they-- they saw some of-- poor people, but very nice people. Some of them rich-- not very few-- a couple. 73% of population was Jewish-- Korets-- the rest was gentile. And the gentile, they live around the city. The city was a-- we had a lot of synagogues-- 17 synagogues. One big one was built by the-- by the-- by a [NON-ENGLISH]-- Polish nobleman-- [NON-ENGLISH] was his name. He build only the-- he build three-- three-- not synagogues-- one synagogue and two churches-- one Polish-Catholic and one-- and-- the other one was not Catholic-- it was orthodox. For each-- each nationality, he build up a church-- a house of worship. And all of them was impacted. This is a-- the-- the Greek orthodox churches, like our-- now museum, because it's so long-- it stays there now. The Jewish synagogue is-- is-- is broken-- burned down. But the-- the Greek orthodox church is still there-- and a monastery near there, with the nuns.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is like a-- I was-- I got a letter from Russia. And there was a-- a stamp with this-- with this church. It's like a-- like a museum now, because it's old. It's a whole thing. So they put upon a stamp.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this little town alone is a beautiful town, with a nice-- nice people, young people. It used to belong to different kind of organizations. And-- but I was young. That time I was around 15-years-old, 14-years-old. I couldn't belong to some of them. But I didn't-- didn't understand yet. I was a very young girl. During the war I start already understanding what it means-- what is war. And in 1939, the war was bombing out. They bombed. The German bombed our town. And then, the Russian came in. So I knew where to start. And, you know, everybody was shaking, bumping you. The airplanes are on top on you. You know what's going on. You never saw a airplane in my life. [LAUGHS] It was the first time I saw it. After that-- that was-- I had some girlfriends, and-- and the same-- the same thing. They-- they all come from orthodox places, homes, raised very Jewish. Shabbat-- it was a Shabbat with a dinner-- special. We used to go visiting each other on the day of Shabbat-- going to synagogue-- all-- all of them raised-- was raised up in the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is like a shtetl, you know? My father used to sit there all the time in the market and-- and take care on making a living. It was not easy, but he tried to mate-- make, but he can't. My mother helped him all the time. He was a grocer?

speaker: interviewee
text: A grocer, yeah-- sending, you know, for the-- he used to come in every-- every-- every once a week it used to be a big market in the city. And the-- all the farmers used to come in and buy things. But a lot of us-- a lot of stores, and my-- my father was not the only one. There was a lot of competition. Everybody used to call in to-- to sell something, to make a living. It was not easy, but he made it, whatnot. You know, we was not rich, but made a living. They tried their best. According to the United States, this was very, very hard. We didn't have these luxuries what we have here. Simple, simple, things we had.

speaker: interviewer
text: But we was happy. Everything was OK. I never complained. My sister, the same thing-- bought a house for herself. My sister lives here in Los Angeles. That time-- I told you already, she was born in-- she younger than I am, four years younger. But so-- what can I say about my-- my youth? It was a Hasidic town. Tell me about Korets in terms of its religious fervor.

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they-- the people was very-- all of them are religious-- very religious people, even my husband's family. I didn't mention that they was religious. It was very religious. His father, his grandfather, was religious people. They used to stay all the time, you know, go down in three times a day to synagogue to pray-- morning, noon, in the-- and in the evening. And they had a rabbi near their house. My house was far from a rabbi, but we was not far from our synagogue. I had-- my father used to belong in that synagogue, and he used to sit in the-- in the-- in the-- in the eastern wall. That was the most-- best, you know, the best places to sit. Because my father was-- And my father was a-- you say it Hebrew, [HEBREW]. All-- every-- every holiday, he used to [HEBREW]. So I used to wear a-- a special-- a special dress, like a white dress on top-- you know, white. And very, very-- very devoted to the Jewish religion-- very devoted. And I was raised up in this city-- in this place. And I'm, up to now, I'm-- I'm religious. I feel like to-- I believe in God-- that spared me. Even if I went through a lot, and I knew we went-- we went through a lot, and I didn't like it, the thing what I went through. Because I felt very guilty. That's why I am a human being. The same thing-- my friends-- and I cannot do the things what my friends-- gentile friends are doing. And I'm not-- and I was-- and even sometimes I was thinking in my head that I would like to be born another animal, not a human being-- during the war. Because that this way-- that day, I couldn't understand this. But I feel like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what do you mean by guilt?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because-- because I was born Jewish. And I felt different-- and orthodox, too. I couldn't eat all the food, what everybody was eating. But during the war, I hide my-- we-- in the hiding place, I eat everything. My mother told me, in a time like that, you-- you don't-- you don't have to be-- choose food. You have to eat whatever somebody gives you. It's a-- it's a-- it's a war time. You have to survive. Eat everything. So I listened to my mother. I eat whatever my-- my-- I did what my mother told me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the war-- when-- would you talk a bit about the town being Hasidic in Kabbalah? You told me something--

speaker: interviewee
text: Kabbalah Hasidism. In this-- that-- that famous rabbi, Rabbi Pinchas Shapiro-- they called him Rabbi [NON-ENGLISH]. He spread all the Hasidism in Kabbalah. It was spread all over around the-- in-- around the whole Poland in nearby neighborhoods. They had a-- a printer shop. They print a hundred books in the-- in-- in the-- over there on this-- about the Kabbalah in Hasidism. So that was the-- the town-- very, very famous town, famous in the history-- written.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you raised Hasidic?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was raised Hasidic, yeah-- orthodox. But I'm not-- now I'm not orthodox. Now, I am conservative. They are new times. You cannot be too orthodox. My son is orthodox.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your daily life like as a girl growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: My daily life? In the morning, I used to say prayers early in the morning. I don't say today; and dress myself up, eat, and go to school. I played with children, like young-- you know, little kids-- run around on the street. It was very, very free there. They did not-- you aren't afraid over there. We didn't-- we wasn't afraid to play there, you know, during Poland's time. It was free. It was not so easy, but we-- in school-- eat, lunch time and supper time, together with the family we eat. My father used to come from work. We used to sit down. Winter was very cold for us-- about 40 degree below 0-- very cold. And my father used to come in just to warm himself up near the oven. There was a oven; and sit down to eat the meals, what my mother used to prepare. My mother used to go there. And in the morning, I used to get up early in the morning-- the snow was up to the knees-- went to school. Everybody, they didn't miss one day to go to school. And I used to help out mother in the house. I used to go and-- it's not like here, you turn off your faucet and you have water running. We used to go-- you had to go bring the water from the-- from the-- they had a pump-- water pump. I used to go with a pail, and pump water, and bring it in the house. This was not easy-- like here. Here is easy in life-- and do the things. And you had to scrape the floors. I helped my-- me and my-- little girls, we has to start scrubbing, cleaning, and washing, and washing the dishes, helping out mother. Mother used to go and help my father to make a living. That was my daily work every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to a religious school in the afternoon?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I went to the religious school. And it was kindergarten. I started right away in the-- in the beginning, when I was five years old-- for religious school. And then I went private-- to religious school after school. Learned.

speaker: interviewer
text: [HEBREW] .

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [HEBREW] We went to learn all the-- the Jewish prayers. In fact, I'm-- when I'm going-- if I go to synagogue here, I pray. I know every-- all-- all the prayers. And that was taught by me by my rabbi, you know, in-- in the-- in my town. We had a yeshiva, too, in our town, a big yeshiva. You know a yeshiva. What will-- this yeshiva have-- Jewish house of learning. All boys are going there. Girls didn't go there-- only boys. Boys used to come in from the different towns. And they didn't have-- we used to station him-- each family used to take a boy for eat-- to eat, because they didn't ever have like, a boarding house.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the families used to take him by day, every day in a different-- different house to eat supper, or eat a lunch, or the breakfast, whatever it is. So the 5,000 Jews used to support the-- a yeshiva. I don't know how many it was. Maybe it was about 100 students there. They used to support them with food. And I think lodging they had over there-- but not food-- sleeping. Were you bat mitzvahed?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The orthodox, they-- it's no bar mitzvah-- bat mitzvah, no. We had a male bar-- bat mitzvahs, but not the-- no, there wasn't bar mitzvah-- bat mitzvah. That's the Jewish-- they teach me all the Jewish things-- what belongs to women, that's all. That-- that's what I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Prepare--

speaker: interviewee
text: Anything--

speaker: interviewee
text: Anything else you want to--

speaker: interviewer
text: --preparing you for--

speaker: interviewee
text: --for a woman-- to preparing me to be a adult. Teaching me-- this was my mother's duty to do it-- to teach me to become a adult. But what-- what is my duties to do-- [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --when I-- for a woman? What's there to do? I'm-- all the duties I'm doing all my life.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] The teaching was not in vain. My mother's teaching was not in vain. Everything-- I am doing whatever I can-- taking care on my family, and learn-- my children went to schools. I took-- we took them to-- through college. Thank God for-- thank God for that. I'm all the time praying to God that I'm still alive. Because my-- I was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --how I want to say this? How do you say this? I was-- sentenced to death in-- in the time from Hitler. And my-- God spared my life. And I am still alive, thank God. 50 years ago, I was sentenced to death. And now, I am still alive. Thank God for that. He spared me-- thanks to good people. We'll talk about that in a minute. Let's cut.
